title: Computer Science
description: Theory and practice of computers and computational systems, including their theory, design, development, and application.

subtopics.title: Number Systems
subtopics.description: Fundamental concept in computer science used to represent and process data using different base numbering systems such as binary, octal, decimal, hexadecimal, and Base64.

subtopics.title: Binary Number System
subtopics.description: Base-2 number system using only 0 and 1; foundational for digital systems and computers.

subtopics.title: Unsigned Binary Arithmetic
subtopics.description: Covers binary addition, subtraction, and overflow handling for unsigned integers.

subtopics.title: Signed Binary Representations
subtopics.description: Explains signed number encodings including sign-and-magnitude, 1’s complement, and 2’s complement.

subtopics.title: Arithmetic and Logical Shifts
subtopics.description: Details the differences and use-cases for arithmetic vs. logical binary shifts.

subtopics.title: Binary Logical Operators
subtopics.description: Covers logic gates and binary operations like AND, OR, XOR, and NOT with real-world examples.

subtopics.title: Floating Point
subtopics.description: Introduces floating-point representation and Negative Powers of Two

subtopics.title: IEEE 754 Format
subtopics.description: Breakdown of IEEE 754 format components including sign, exponent, and mantissa.

subtopics.title: Octal Number System
subtopics.description: Base-8 number system using digits 0 through 7; sometimes used in digital electronics.

subtopics.title: Hexadecimal Number System
subtopics.description: Base-16 system using digits 0–9 and letters A–F; commonly used in memory addressing and color codes in computing.

subtopics.title: Decimal System and Base Conversion via Division and Remainders
subtopics.description: Understanding the base-10 number system and how repeated division and remainders enable conversion to binary, octal, and hexadecimal.

subtopics.title: Base64 Encoding
subtopics.description: A binary-to-text encoding scheme that represents binary data using 64 ASCII characters; widely used in data transmission, such as email and image embedding in HTML.

subtopics.title: Cryptology
subtopics.description: Cryptology is the rigorous science of designing, analyzing, and breaking secure information systems, comprising both cryptography and cryptanalysis.

subtopics.title: Cryptography
subtopics.description: Cryptography is the rigorous science of securing information against the adversarial behaviour.

subtopics.title: Classic Cryptography
subtopics.description: Cryptographic methods predating modern mathematical approaches, typically using manual or mechanical ciphers to obscure messages.

subtopics.title: Classical Cipher Properties
subtopics.description: Overview of classical ciphers, their mathematical foundations, and principles of confusion, diffusion, and analysis.

subtopics.title: Classic Transposition Cipher
subtopics.description: A transposition cipher encrypts a message by rearranging the positions of its characters according to a secret permutation without altering the characters themselves; classic examples include the Rail Fence, Columnar, Double Columnar, and Scytale ciphers.

subtopics.title: Classic Substitution Cipher
subtopics.description: A substitution cipher encrypts a message by replacing each character with another according to a secret mapping or key without changing the character positions; classic examples include the Caesar, Atbash, Vigenère, and Playfair ciphers.

subtopics.title: Modern Cryptography
subtopics.description: Cryptography based on formal mathematical principles and computational assumptions, providing provable security against well-defined adversaries in digital systems.

subtopics.title: Shannon Perfect Secrecy
subtopics.description: Shannon's concept of perfect secrecy states that a ciphertext provides absolutely no information about the original plaintext, meaning the probability of the plaintext remains the same whether you've seen the ciphertext or not.

subtopics.title: Adversarial Model
subtopics.description: An adversary model in cryptography is a formalized definition of a potential attacker, specifying their capabilities, goals, and limitations to rigorously evaluate the security of a cryptographic protocol or system.

subtopics.title: Cryptographic Oracles
subtopics.description: An oracle is often described as a "black box" because the attacker doesn't need to know the internal workings of the system.

subtopics.title: Cryptanalysis
subtopics.description: Cryptanalysis is the study of breaking or evaluating cryptographic systems to find weaknesses.

subtopics.title: Programming
subtopics.description: Writing and understanding code.

subtopics.title: Python
subtopics.description: Beginner-friendly programming language.

subtopics.title: C++
subtopics.description: High-performance language for systems and games.
